photos.title: Projector
photos.description: Projector light often tricks camera sensors into seeing purple due to underexposure and metamerism (where near-UV light activates red pixels). High-contrast scenes can also cause chromatic aberration that adds purple fringing around bright blues.

photos.title: Blood Moon
photos.description: The term “blood moon” refers to its reddish orange appearance, occurring during a total lunar eclipse. When the earth passes between the sun and moon, the little sunlight not blocked by the earth managing to reach the lunar surface is filtered through the edges of the Earth’s atmosphere. 🌎 While Earth’s air molecules scatter most of the blue (shorter) wavelengths of light, reddish tones (longer wavelengths) are left behind reflecting off of the moon and casting a red glow that makes it appear red.
photos.camera: 📸 The Moon Godess’s Canon EOS Rebel t6 🌒

photos.title: Growth

photos.title: White Sands
photos.description: 1 of the 2 national parks in New Mexico. White Sands interestingly is one of the few deserts in the world with gypsum sand, a rare type of sand that's water soluble. Even more rare is finding gypsum sand in the form of dunes such as those found at our beloved national park!
photos.location: White Sands National Park, NM - The Chihuahuan Desert

photos.title: Eclipse
photos.description: This total solar eclipse's path of totality actually ended up passing through ~15 different states! The next time the U.S. will see a total solar eclipse will be in 2044 & 2045 (other than the one Alaska will get to see in 2033).
photos.camera: 📸 The Sun God's 🌞 Canon EOS Rebel t6, 75-300mm Lens

photos.title: Tracks
photos.description: This area of land was originally inhabited by the Mescalero Apache for thousands of years, before Spanish and later American settlers introduced ranching, mining, and logging in the 16th–19th centuries. During the late 1800s, the expansion of railroads in southern New Mexico accelerated large-scale logging in the region, as timber was harvested to supply railroad construction and nearby mining operations. It was formally established in 1902 as the Lincoln Forest Reserve and later expanded and consolidated into a single national forest through federal actions between 1908 and 1917.
photos.camera: 📸 Theodore Roosevelt's 🌲 Canon EOS Rebel t6

photos.title: Downtime
photos.description: A quick pic of the road out to the rocket rails at the IREC 2024 competition.
photos.location: Spaceport America - Just outside of Truth or Consequences, NM (yes that is the real city name).

photos.title: Eclipse 2
photos.description: This is another pic of the 2024 total solar eclipse. Despite being a little blurry (that's the farthest my camera at the time could zoom), I wanted to include this picture because you can see just a few hints of the solar prominences. These are brief flashes of red light appearing on the rim of the moon which are actually these massive loops (typically ~35x larger than earth) of plasma shooting out from the sun's surface. They appear red because their hot hydrogen gas emits light at a specific red wavelength (called H-alpha) when its electrons lose energy. You usually can't see them because of how bright the sun is, but with the moon masking the photosphere, this is a rare and special moment where you can actually see them with the naked eye.
photos.camera: 📸 Ra's ☀️ Canon EOS Rebel t6, 75-300mm Lens

photos.title: Forest
photos.description: Another shot of the Lincoln National Forest. The 2001 Roadless Rule established prohibitions on road construction, road reconstruction, and timber harvesting in nearly 60 million acres inventoried roadless areas, including the Lincoln National Forest (USDA Forest Service). In January and March of 2025, Executive Order 14192 (Prosperity Through Deregulation) and Executive Order 14225 (Immediate Expansion of American Timber Production) were passed. From a high-level overview, Executive Order 14192 basically told agencies they must give up 10 regulations if they want to issue 1 new one. One stipulation is that all new rules must cost 'significantly less than zero,' so (Cost of New Rules Passed) - (Savings from Old Rules Deleted) = Total, which must be a large negative number. This incentivizes agencies to include 'costly' rules to be cut, or rather, rules that could potentially make a lot of money. Many experts identify the Roadless Rule to be one of the rules likely to be cut as its protections on logging has the potential to make the logging industry millions of dollars, despite the rule actually currently saving the government money by not having to construct and maintain more roads, among other things. Additionally, Executive Order 14225 orders the rescission of any policies that burden timber production and authorizing 'categorical exclusions' to bypass environmental reviews, which would lead to immediate road construction and industrial logging in previously untouched wilderness. While these bills must first go through a formal public process to fully repeal the 2001 Roadless Rules, they represent intent and mechanism for future destruction. The actual physical logging of these forests would only begin after the Forest Service completes the lengthy legal process of rescinding the rule. Stay informed!!
photos.camera: 📸 Theodore Roosevelt's (currently rolling in his grave) Canon EOS Rebel t6

photos.description: Rock

photos.title: Project: Landscape - Road
photos.description: We begin with an empty road curving into the distance at night. The aerial perspective of this photograph captures a unique point of view just barely below eye level with the closest of the street lights. Looking further into the distance, we’re able to see layers of entities, trailing past the road, beyond a bridge, toward what appears to be a residential area, followed by office buildings further into the background, and finally followed by the silhouettes of faint powerline towers. As our eyes follow the path of streetlights, we see sections of the road split up into patches of evenly spaced light and shadows. To me, this is the beginning of a journey, down a road of uncertainty. Because we’re seeing the space from a slightly higher and more encompassing perspective, we see the clutter of signage covering the sidelines of the road. This could be overwhelming, seeing this path from a larger perspective or seeing the big picture, realizing just how much left there is to go, whereas if you were to drive on the road, you would be directed by one sign at a time. The yellow and orange tones create a feeling of warmth on the path ahead, with the outer areas of the photograph concealed in darkness and unsureness. I really love the yellow tones of this image as it can convey several feelings dependent on the explorer. While perhaps a more vibrant yellow during the day could express joy, excitement and more, the opposite is captured here. I wouldn’t say it’s completely doom and gloom necessarily, but there is a sense of naivety (with still a bit of novelty) that I tried to capture to mix in with the uncertainty around the corner. Additionally, I have always associated this shade of yellow with a sense of insanity, in reference to Charlotte Perkins Gilman’s short story “The Yellow Wallpaper.” While I wouldn’t say the beginning of this photograph’s story is as intense, I still connected some sensation of slight insanity derived from some of the feelings I think many students go through during their time at university (fitting for utd).

photos.title: Project: Landscape - Pegasus
photos.description: My third and final image in this series explores a vast corner of downtown Dallas, taken from the pool balcony of the Omni hotel. Almost a century ago, a bright red neon pegasus was fixed on top of the Magnolia Oil Company’s headquarters. Eventually, the company was acquired by another, moving the pegasus into storage. However, while a new upgraded version of the pegasus was eventually installed (and can be seen in the top right corner of the image) the original piece was revitalized and placed back on display in 2015. This pegasus has always been a symbol to welcome visitors, and as a Greek mythological creature, it has also been known to present wealth, power and fame. I really love this idea because as the last image in this series, the viewer can imagine this as the final destination, or perhaps a checkpoint on their continuing journey. The pegasus may represent a goal, dream, or even a fantasy (for better or for worse). Regardless, the vibrant red and glowing focal point captures the viewer’s eye, with a few people below the pegasus’s hooves providing even more of a larger-than-life sense of scale. At first I was frustrated at being unable to capture a sharper image of the pegasus as it was in a constant state of rotation. However, I found that the slow movement added a nice fuzzy glow that didn’t need to be perfected and actually ended up adding to the eye-catching center. Additionally, there are still empty roads in the foreground, but now seem to crisscross and cover much larger spaces with several more options for paths to choose and follow (including underground). However, notice how the roads are no longer completely empty. A convoy of late-night construction workers crossed by a line of straggling SUV’s now fill parts of the roads, though far from the clutter of rush hour. From these roads to the towering buildings climbing up the left side of the background, there are many pairs of both horizontal, angled, and vertical leading lines. These lines separate parts of the concrete jungle, with sections of varying directions in light and windows pulling the eye from one building to the next. If not for the bright red pegasus in the center along with the skyline above, it would be difficult to differentiate building from building. Despite an overall sense of clutter and choice, I also want to point out a particularly shiny building toward the right of the image. This building is reflecting the lights from the Omni hotel, exposing a smooth-textured and window-covered office building. I love this layer in particular because of how it adds yet another element of reflection, both in a physical and metaphorical sense. The viewer may choose to stay here, or they may choose to continue down a new road, potentially leading to even more areas of interest.

photos.title: Project: Landscape - Gate
photos.description: We see a geometric square of negative space playing the role of a gate-like transitional zone. This entrance surrounds a pathway leading further into a dark area in the distance. Above the square gate, there’s an indoor bridge concealed in glass lit up with fluorescent lights, but inaccessible to the current viewer’s location. While there are minimal points of light, there’s a relatively even sense of symmetry and gravity between light fixtures, trees, and small signs on the side. Because the photograph was taken centered to the path, the sides of the path act as leading lines with its corners leading from the bottom left and right corners of the photo toward the center and into the darkness beyond. To me, this photo shows how while on a journey, you may know exactly where you need to go (down the path) or at least have an idea of what that looks like, but that doesn’t diminish how scary or anxietous following that path may feel. The road beyond the gate isn’t even completely concealed, but the fear of the unknown and again, the recurrence of uncertainty, plague the viewer’s mind as they imagine taking their next steps.

photos.title: Project: Abstract - Lush
photos.description: With this project, I wanted to see if I could portray an idea or concept somewhat related to time, or at least in this case, an evolution of sorts. Moving from an organic and relatively natural space, to a sharp and confusing visual in movement, to a stagnant and more-defined image. In terms of the steps I took to communicate this, I paid close attention to the perspective from which I took these photographs. The top being looking down, the second uncertain, and the third looking up. I also made sure the colors made sense in my head with what I might associate with the ideas being portrayed. Starting with the objective visuals, the first photograph pictures a lush and soft landscape. I decided to bring abstract elements into this photograph by picturing that land as if it were another planet. This photo was actually taken at the state park Enchanting Rock, just north of Fredericksburg, Texas. Capturing this space from this perspective, I wanted the viewer to have to look twice to confirm whether the patches of grass were closer or further from the camera, perhaps from an aerial view. I also love that I was able to capture my sister way out in the background on what seems to be the horizon, but then noticing that there’s a forest or greenery covering beyond that, further adding to the distortion of scale and perspective, as if the land almost curves up like in one of the final scenes of the film Interstellar.

photos.title: Project: Abstract - Sharp
photos.description: Moving on to the second image, the contents of this are much harder to decipher. With this photo, I wanted to convey an aura of sharpness and chaos with a slight duality of flowing softness between the separations. This photo was taken in the mirror room at the Grapevine location of Meow Wolf, a large-scale interactive and immersive art installation. Within this room, projectors play a loop of a sky from dusk until dawn within the span of about 15 minutes. There are large reflective geometric shapes and prisms that span the entire room. Because all sides of each specially-placed shape are covered in reflective surfaces, taking a photograph from any perspective would present multiple views and reflections of the room. I loved the obvious separation between shapes, but simultaneously not-so-obvious reflections on said shapes. On one side, you can see a distorted reflection of my sister’s silhouette, while directly below you can see what seems to be a cloud, but it’s not entirely obvious. Toward the upper/middle right, there’s also a ripple-like reflection that blends opposing surfaces.

photos.title: Proejct: Abstract - Structure
photos.description: Lastly, with my third and final image, I wanted to capture rigidness and structure. I remember thanking the weather this day for being cloudy as it basically turned the sky into a light box. This photo is of just the top part of a newly-built home in a new housing development in Austin, Texas. I remember debating on whether or not I wanted to include more of the house, but ultimately deciding I liked the sharp yet defined corners of the roof specifically. I also decided that including more of the house would take away from potential implications or meanings pulled from the rest of the image as it would provide more context than necessary. Additionally, I really love how the sky was clear and relatively monotone on this day, allowing more emphasis to fall on the minimal shadows and drawing the eyes toward the bottom of the image with the weight or gravity of attention focusing on what little is provided.

photos.title: Project: Final - Streetlight
photos.description: With my final project, I wanted to explore the theme of what we leave behind. Whether that be literal physical infrastructure, or less tangible things like childhood, relationships, lessons learned, or people we once were. Through this project I kind of ended up in an introspective mindset, especially with it being the end of the semester. I hope these images are not only visually appealing, but also encapsulate a story, with design elements aiding in that narrative. - With this first image, I really tried to frame it geometrically with surrounding objects and lines. Centering and leveling each line guides the viewer’s eyes toward the center light source which subsequently is also framed by the reflection of color on the organics around it. While there’s only one light post visible in the frame, shadows and lights on the ground indicate there are other lights nearby, avoiding the feeling of complete isolation. If you want to read into it even further, the repetition of posts lining the sidewalk may indicate a selection of choices guiding one toward varying directions, though one could also just view them as visually appealing.

photos.title: Project: Final - Laundry and Taxes
photos.description: With this image, we visit one of those quintessential American hotspots: the laundromat. This is a space fairly recognizable that many people pass through on a daily basis (though there’s something to say on economic class and who’s visiting the laundromat vs. who has a washer/dryer at home). Here, one could consider the literal laundry they’re leaving behind, or the memories, thoughts, relationships, etc. that they wash away at the end of the day. Additionally, one may even consider the stains that you never can quite get out of your favorite t-shirt, or the white bed sheets that are now stained pink forever. Looking more at some of the design elements in this composition, we see plenty of leading lines, a bit of glare, reflections, and plenty of repetition throughout the machines, pillars, fans, ceiling tiles, and more.

photos.title: Project: Final - Lecture
photos.description: This image is pretty straightforward I think at least in terms of what the space is and what it’s used for. However, I think depending on the viewer’s varying background and experiences, education, especially higher education can mean a lot of different things to different people. For one, we can look at the design elements of the curvature of seats all pointing toward the front of the lecture hall with the ceiling tiles reflecting those patterns, providing a relatively visually appealing space overall. Tying it back to the theme, we can think about the academic lessons learned, but also the naivety, child, or sometimes immaturity that many of us leave behind in this next stage of life (though not always of course). Even though we’re always growing and changing as people, I think many of us can agree that how you spend your time as a young adult is one of the major factors that shapes who you turn into or what kind of person you’ll be (for the most part) for the rest of your life. So in this image, I imagine the viewer reflecting on how they changed in college if they attended one, the challenges and hardships they faced, and how starkly they changed and evolved as a person. Perhaps on the flip side, one could imagine the viewer reflecting on the opportunities regarding higher education they were not afforded, or how they may have chosen to pivot away from in favor of a different path.

photos.title: Project: Final - Lab
photos.description: This image again kind of follows a specific path that may not be as universal, but still alludes to an overall experience or sentiment I think many can relate to. This is a photo of a lab in one of our neuro/nanotech labs. While academic research may be a relatively niche career path that many people in the world don’t pursue or often even have access to as an opportunity, it’s still an example of the contributions we make to society, either as an undergrad, graduate student, professor, or even just industry professionals. This image specifically shows a space that’s lived in and used. Lots of tiny details throughout the room provide a sense of depth, though many things aren’t immediately recognizable or straightforward especially if this area of STEM isn't your expertise. I like this room also because of how it can signify exploration and discovery. Part of the human experience I think heavily rests on the idea that we are explorers, ideators, and innovators. The continual advancement of society as a result of our combined efforts I think is unique and special, providing purpose, excitement, and embodying the human spirit.

photos.title: Project: Final - Next
photos.description: Last but not least, this final image pictures the entrance to what appears to be a dark tunnel (though it’s actually a bridge that the train passes over). Throughout the semester I played with deadpan visuals and aesthetics a lot. This last image ties back to bits and pieces of all of my other projects, especially through the liminal space. The first project we did with landscape photography, I explored a theme of direction, journey, and uncertainty. By playing with the manual camera settings I tried to capture similar sentiments to that here, but this time tying it into my final project theme. While the image is dark, dim, and mysterious, the actual scene pictures weathered infrastructure, overgrown plants, and a clear road around a corner. There’s signs of people having been there, with an old leftover construction cone, a flood gauge, and reflective sign at the corner. These traces of people show that this road has in fact been traveled before, so despite how scary it might be to take the next step forward, especially without knowing what’s ahead, we know that the next step is not impossible because it’s been done before. Compared to the first image, even though there were guiding poles and there’s less light illuminating the area, everything seemed relatively new (this is because it was literally in a new development). But by reframing our mindset and perspective around this final image, we can take that uncertainty and instead reassure ourselves that around that corner there will still be something, and it’s up to us to take that leap of faith and continue forward.

photos.title: Project: Wide Format - Hall
photos.description: 5 Design Elements/Choices: (1) Leading Lines: For this project I really wanted to lean into capturing leading lines from a certain perspective. While there weren’t a lot of objects on this floor or in this space, there were still plenty of different compositional elements that add to the photograph. It’s almost along the lines of the presence of absence, with almost nothing in the room, but so much detail that it captures the viewer’s eye regardless. - (2) Curved Lines (lens curve): At first I considered trying to straighten out the small lens curve, but then I thought it added a nice touch to the space, making it seem almost distorted perhaps like in a dream or even a nightmare down a long dark hall. - (3) Colors: I really love how for the most part this entire photo is in grayscale with the exception of the green exit signs. Because the signs are virtually the only pop of color, it’s one of the first elements that stands out, until the viewer follows them down the line into the distance only to realize the illusion of an end but not quite being able to see a definitive end of the hall. - (4) Brightness: I also really liked how the lighting and brightness came out in this photo, with the front section being lit up by a bright fluorescent above, but immediately losing a strong light source the further down the hall you go. The lack of or absence of light the deeper into the photo you go, the more is left to the unknown and the more there is for the viewer to imagine what might be in the darkness the farther down you go. - (4) Movement - repetition: The repetition of exit signs added a nice touch to the photo. While they’re not only visually appealing, they also add another layer with them supposedly leading someone to the exit, but the exit not actually being in sight forcing the viewer to keep pushing to find the end. The repetition also adds a nice sense of scale and depth to the photo, especially with the reflection of their lights following the floor and ceiling down the line and into the darkness.
